title: Chapter 10: The Prophecy Unfolds
content: The storm broke over Ironmoon Castle that evening.

Thunder rolled across the Blackwood Forest like the growl of some ancient beast. Lightning split the sky in silver-white forks that matched the color of Sera's fur. Rain hammered against the castle walls with the force of a thousand tiny fists.
In Elder Magnus's tower, three wolves sat around a circle of candlelight.

"The prophecy is more detailed than I initially revealed," Magnus said, spreading the ancient scroll across a low table. The parchment crackled with age, its edges burned and frayed, but the script at its center remained clear. "I wanted to be certain before I shared the rest."
"Certain of what?" Kael asked.

Magnus's pale eyes moved between them. "That you were the ones it spoke of. The Alpha of Two Hearts. The Silver Queen. The prophecy has waited centuries for its subjects to appear. I needed to be sure it wasn't... wishful thinking."
*"Wishful thinking? Is he saying he's been HOPING we'd show up? For how long?! How old IS this wolf?!"*
"Elder Magnus has been studying the old texts for fifty years," Sera translated. "He's been waiting for us."
Magnus's beard twitched. "The mind-link has its uses, I see." He tapped the scroll. "The full text reads as follows:

*When the pack is broken and the shadow rises,
When the Alpha's heart is split in twain,
Then shall the Moon-Marked maiden awaken,
Born under the dying crescent's pain.

She who hears the unspoken thunder,
She whose wolf was sealed by fear,
Shall bridge the divide and heal the fracture,
And stand beside the Alpha, without peer.

The Silver Queen and the Two-Hearted King,
Together shall face the darkness that comes,
Their bond shall be the pack's salvation,
When the howling of the shadow drums.

But beware the false wolf in silken clothing,
Beware the blood that claims the throne,
For the path to the crown is paved with betrayal,
And the Queen must claim what is her own.*"
The words settled into the candlelit silence like stones dropping into still water.
"That's... ominous," Sera said.

"Prophecies usually are." Magnus rolled the scroll carefully. "The key points are these: the pack faces an existential threat — 'the shadow.' The Alpha's fractured nature — his war with his inner wolf — is both a weakness and, eventually, a strength. The Moon-Marked maiden — you, Seraphina — is the bridge that will heal him."
"And the false wolf in silken clothing?" Kael's voice was dangerously quiet.

"Isolde, almost certainly. 'The blood that claims the throne' — her family has been angling for the Alpha seat for generations." Magnus's eyes grew distant. "But there is one line that troubles me more than the others."
"'The Queen must claim what is her own,'" Sera quoted. "Meaning... I have to actively take the throne? It won't just be given to me?"

"In the old days, a Wolf Queen was not crowned. She *claimed* her place — through trial, through combat, through an act so profound that the pack could not deny her worthiness." Magnus's gaze was heavy. "The prophecy suggests you will face a similar test."

*"A trial. She has to pass a TRIAL. What kind of trial? Combat? She's strong but she's been a wolf for less than a WEEK. Riddles? She's brilliant but prophecies don't usually test RIDDLES. Sacrifice? NO. Absolutely not. No sacrifices. I refuse. Whatever this trial is, I'm doing it WITH her."*
"The trial doesn't have to be something she does alone," Sera said, translating the thought before Kael could voice it. "Fenris is already planning to help."
Magnus's eyebrows rose. "You can hear the inner wolf as a separate voice?"
"Fenris," Sera confirmed. "He's... distinct."
"He's a disaster," Kael muttered. "But he's mine."

*"He called me 'his.' He's never called me 'his' before. I've always been 'the wolf' or 'the inner voice' or 'that ridiculous creature who won't stop composing poetry.' But 'HIS.' I'm HIS. I'm going to — "*
"Fenris is crying again," Sera reported.
"Of course he is."

Magnus watched this exchange with an expression of profound fascination. "The integration has already begun. Your outer and inner selves are starting to merge — to acknowledge each other. This is... ahead of schedule."
"What schedule?"

"The prophecy's timeline. I expected it would take months — perhaps years — for the Alpha of Two Hearts to reconcile with his inner wolf. But something has accelerated the process." Magnus's pale eyes fixed on Sera. "Something — or someone — has given him a reason to change."
Sera felt her face heat.

*"She's blushing. She's BLUSHING. Because of US. Because we're changing. For HER. We're changing for her and she KNOWS it and she's BLUSHING. This is the greatest moment of my existence. I'm going to remember this blush forever. I'm going to write a poem about this blush. 'Her cheeks are roses and I am the thorn that — no, that's terrible. That makes me sound like a VILLAIN. Start over.'"*

"The integration is important," Magnus continued. "The prophecy says 'she shall bridge the divide and heal the fracture.' The more whole you become, Kael, the stronger both of you will be. And the stronger Sera's wolf will grow in response."
"Because our wolves are linked," Sera said. "Mirrors. Counterparts."

"Exactly. The Goddess didn't just choose you randomly. She chose you because your wolves were made for each other. Two halves of a whole that was divided long ago — perhaps before either of you were born."
The candlelight flickered. Thunder rumbled outside.
"When the shadow comes," Magnus said quietly, "you will need to be at full strength. Both of you. Together."
After the session with Magnus, Kael walked Sera back to her quarters in the servant wing.
"You should move to the noble quarters," he said, his voice carefully neutral. "You're not a servant anymore. You haven't been for a while."
"The servants are my people." Sera's voice was quiet. "I've lived among them my whole life. Moving to the noble wing would feel like abandoning them."
"Then I'll improve the servant quarters."
"You can't just — "
"I'm the Alpha. I can do whatever I want." His lips curved slightly. "Within reason. Fenris would like me to note that 'within reason' is a very broad category."

*"IT IS. EXTREMELY BROAD. We could fill her room with flowers. Lavender. LOTS of lavender. Wait, does she actually LIKE lavender or do I just ASSOCIATE lavender with her because of the soap she uses? I should ask. I'm going to ask. Kael, ask her about the lavender."*
"Do you actually like lavender," Kael said, "or is that just the soap the servants use?"

Sera laughed — a real laugh, surprised and bright. "It's just the soap. I don't have a particular attachment to lavender. I do, however, have an attachment to not having flowers fill my room because I'm mildly allergic to most of them."

*"SHE'S ALLERGIC TO FLOWERS. I almost filled her room with FLOWERS. I almost POISONED HER with ROMANCE. This is why we ask questions. This is why communication is IMPORTANT. Writing that down. Mental note: no flowers. Gifts must be NON-ALLERGENIC."*
"What else should I know?" Kael asked. "About you. Things the link can't tell me."
Sera paused at her door. "That's a broad question."
"I have time."

The corridor was empty. The storm was still raging outside. And Kael Thornwood — the Wolf King, the cold Alpha, the man who had once said she was a stain upon the pack — was standing in the servant quarters, asking to know her.
"My favorite color is silver," Sera said. "Not because of my wolf. I've liked silver since I was a child. It's the color of moonlight on water. The color of hope in the dark."
Kael listened.
"I'm afraid of deep water. I almost drowned when I was seven — one of the pack children pushed me into the river as a 'joke.' I've never learned to swim."
His jaw tightened, but he didn't interrupt.
"I talk to myself when I'm nervous. Full conversations. Out loud. It's embarrassing and I try not to do it in front of people."
"I've noticed," Kael said. "You were muttering about tea leaves this morning."
"That was a different thing."
"What thing?"
"I was trying to figure out if you'd prefer Earl Grey or Darjeeling and I was arguing with myself about the merits of each."
Kael's expression flickered — surprise, then something warmer. "You argue with yourself about my tea preferences?"
"Your inner wolf has strong opinions about tea. He thinks Darjeeling is 'pretentious' and Earl Grey is 'the tea of warriors.' I was trying to decide whether to respect his opinion or ignore it."
*"I HAVE BEEN HEARD. MY OPINIONS HAVE BEEN CONSIDERED. She was going to ignore my tea opinions but she STILL CONSIDERED THEM. This is more validation than I've received in my ENTIRE EXISTENCE."*
"Darjeeling tomorrow," Kael said. "Fenris can deal with it."
"Really?"
"He's been insufferable all week. A little suffering will be good for his character."

*"I HEARD THAT. You're punishing me for being HAPPY. This is discrimination against inner wolves. I'm filing a complaint. With... someone. The Moon Goddess. I'm filing a complaint with the Moon Goddess."*
Sera laughed again — and then the laugh caught in her throat because Kael had stepped closer, his body blocking the torchlight, his golden eyes burning down at her.
"What else?" he murmured.
"I'm..." She swallowed. "I'm terrified that this is all a dream. That I'm going to wake up tomorrow and be the wolf-less maid again. That you're going to realize I'm not worth the trouble."
Kael's hand rose to cup her face — the gesture was becoming familiar now, as natural as breathing.

"You transformed into a silver wolf. You killed two rogues. You interrogated twenty-three servants and found a traitor in under an hour. You've been in my head for weeks and instead of running away, you stayed." His thumb traced her cheekbone. "You are not a dream, Seraphina Vale. You are the most real thing that has ever happened to me."
The thunder rolled. The rain hammered. And Sera kissed him — not brief this time, not gentle, but deep and hungry and full of all the things she couldn't say yet.
When they broke apart, Kael's breathing was ragged.
"That's four," he said hoarsely.
"Four what?"
"Four times you've kissed me. I'm keeping count."

*"FIVE if you count the jaw kiss. Do we count the jaw kiss? I count the jaw kiss. That makes FIVE. Six if you count the one that's about to happen. Is there about to be another one? There should definitely be another one."*
"Five," Sera corrected, her voice breathless. "The jaw counts."
"The jaw counts," Kael agreed, and kissed her again.
Somewhere in the storm, a wolf howled — not a real wolf, but something older. Something watching. Something that was running out of time.
Neither of them heard it.
They were too busy counting.
